coredata.dc:title: A separated type cathode photoelectrochemical aptasensor for thrombin detection based on novel organic polymer heterojunction photoelectric material
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Ya
Wang, Baoying
Wang, Jingjing
Guo, Qingfu
Yang, Xiaoyan
Nie, Guangming
coredata.dc:description: In this work, poly(5-formylindole)/poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (P5FIn/PEDOT) organic polymer heterojunction was firstly prepared and utilized as a novel photocathode material to construct a separated type cathode photoelectrochemical (STC-PEC) aptasensor for ultrasensitive and selective detection of thrombin (TB). Specifically, P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction and TB aptamers in this PEC sensor were modified on different ITO electrodes to make the photoexcitation and biological recognition process separately. P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction was prepared by simple two-step electrochemical polymerization and exhibited a strong photocurrent signal owing to the synergistic effect between P5FIn and PEDOT. This photocathode need not to be replaced in the PEC measurement improving the reproducibility. This construction strategy of the proposed sensor avoided the mutual interference between photoexcitation process and biological detection process, and effectively improved the sensitivity, reproducibility and stability of this PEC sensor. The proposed STC-PEC aptasensor had a detection range of 0.000100 nM–10.0 nM with a detection limit of 0.0410 pM, also showing an excellent applicability in the detection of TB in real human serum samples. This STC-PEC aptasensor not only has a good potential application in future clinical medical diagnosis and disease treatment but also provides new ideas for the development of PEC sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemistry
Photocathode aptasensor
Poly(5-formylindole)
Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)
Organic heterojunction
Thrombin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of P5FIn/PEDOT organic polymer heterojunction
Analytical performance of the STC-PEC aptasensor for TB
Stability, selectivity and reproducibility of STC-PEC aptasensor
Application of STC-PEC aptasensor in real human serum samples detection

National Natural Science Foundation of China

Qingdao University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51973102); Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2019MB067); Qinghai Provincial Basic Research Program (2021-ZJ-710); Open Foundation of State Key Laboratory Base for Eco-chemical Engineering (STHG2003); and Talent Fund of QUST (2020).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) erGO, (B) PEDOT, (C) P5FIn and (D) P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction.

CV of 5FIn monomer on (A) bare ITO electrode and (B) PEDOT/ITO electrode. (C) First CV loop of 5FIn on (a) bare ITO electrode and (b) PEDOT/ITO electrode. (D) CV of (a) P5FIn, (b) PEDOT and (c) P5FIn/PEDOT (scan rate: 50 mV s−1).
(A) Photocurrent responses of (a) PEDOT/ITO, (b) P5FIn/ITO and (c) P5FIn/PEDOT/ITO. (B) Schematic illustration of electron transfer for STC-PEC aptasensor.

(A) The photocurrent response of bare ITO electrode (a) and P5FIn/PEDOT/ITO electrode (b) when erGO/ITO was used as counter electrode; The photocurrent response of the sensor after the erGO/ITO electrode incubated with aptamers (c) and further incubated with 0.0100 nM TB (d). (B) EIS of (a) erGO/ITO electrode, (b) aptamer/erGO/ITO electrode and (c) aptamer/erGO/ITO electrode incubated with TB.
Photocurrent response of (A) erGO/ITO electrode incubated with varied concentrations of aptamers, (B) different incubation time of TB on aptamers/erGO/ITO electrode.

(A) Photocurrent responses of the proposed STC-PEC aptasensor for detecting different concentrations of TB ((a) 100 fM, (b) 500 fM, (c) 1.00 pM, (d) 5.00 pM, (e) 10.0 pM, (f) 50.0 pM, (g) 100 pM, (h) 500 pM, (i) 1.00 nM, (j) 5.00 nM and (k) 10.0 nM), (B) Calibration curve of this PEC sensor to different TB concentrations.

(A) Stability of sensor under light ON/OFF for 10 cycles, (B) Photocurrent responses of the sensor for blank solution, CEA (10.0 nM), BSA (10.0 nM), IgG (10.0 nM), TB (0.0100 nM), their mixture (interferon concentration is 10.0 nM), (C) Repeatability of two groups electrodes (5/group) to test the different concentrations of TB (0.0100 nM, 0.100 nM).
Construction process of the separation type cathode photoelectrochemical aptasensor (STC-PEC) based on P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction.
Comparison of STC-PEC aptasensor with other detection TB sensor.
Application of STC-PEC sensor in actual serum samples.

head.ce:title: A separated type cathode photoelectrochemical aptasensor for thrombin detection based on novel organic polymer heterojunction photoelectric material

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, poly(5-formylindole)/poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (P5FIn/PEDOT) organic polymer heterojunction was firstly prepared and utilized as a novel photocathode material to construct a separated type cathode photoelectrochemical (STC-PEC) aptasensor for ultrasensitive and selective detection of thrombin (TB). Specifically, P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction and TB aptamers in this PEC sensor were modified on different ITO electrodes to make the photoexcitation and biological recognition process separately. P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction was prepared by simple two-step electrochemical polymerization and exhibited a strong photocurrent signal owing to the synergistic effect between P5FIn and PEDOT. This photocathode need not to be replaced in the PEC measurement improving the reproducibility. This construction strategy of the proposed sensor avoided the mutual interference between photoexcitation process and biological detection process, and effectively improved the sensitivity, reproducibility and stability of this PEC sensor. The proposed STC-PEC aptasensor had a detection range of 0.000100 nM–10.0 nM with a detection limit of 0.0410 pM, also showing an excellent applicability in the detection of TB in real human serum samples. This STC-PEC aptasensor not only has a good potential application in future clinical medical diagnosis and disease treatment but also provides new ideas for the development of PEC sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Thrombin (TB), as a kind of serine protease with a blood coagulation effect, can participate in coagulation cascade to convert soluble fibrinogen into insoluble fibrin, promote blood coagulation, and facilitate the activation and aggregation of platelet [1-3]. But abnormally expressed TB is related to thrombosis which can lead to a variety of cardiovascular diseases, such as atherosclerosis, stroke and myocardial infarction [4,5]. Therefore, the accurate detection of TB content has great significance in disease prevention and treatment.

Compared with some reported methods for TB detection (such as colorimetric [6], immunoassay [7], surface enhanced Raman scattering [8], and electrochemistry [2]), photoelectrochemical (PEC) assay is a promising analytical technique, which utilizes the photoinduced current signals to detect the analyte and has the features of low background, fast detection speed, low cost and high sensitivity [9–12]. At present, there are two main categories of PEC biosensors: cathodic PEC sensing platforms and anodic PEC sensing platforms. In the detection of real biological samples with complex components, the presence of reducing substances such as ascorbic acid, polypeptide, enzyme and dopamine will disturb the inherent hole oxidation at the photoanode interface, resulting in unreliable photocurrent signal generated by PEC sensor [13]. In contrast, the photocathode sensing platform takes holes as the primary carriers and transfers electrons into electron acceptors at the electrode–electrolyte surface, which has stronger anti-interference ability to reductive agents and has gradually become the focus of attention [14–16].

P-type inorganic semiconductors and their heterojunctions are commonly used as photosensitive materials to construct photocathodic sensing platforms [17–19]. However, the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, poor conductivity and biological toxicity hinder their practical application [20]. Organic conducting polymers (OCPs) (polyaniline [21], polyindole [22], polythiophene [23] and polypyrrole [24]) have been gradually used in PEC sensors as organic semiconductor photosensitive materials due to their fast electron transfer, low cost and excellent photoelectric conversion performance [25]. Poly(5-formylindole) (P5FIn) with good electrical conductivity and biocompatibility is a new member of polyindole family. More importantly, the stability and photoelectric properties of P5FIn is further improved due to the introduction of aldehyde group, which makes P5FIn a promising photoelectric material [25]. However, the application of pure P5FIn in PEC sensor is still restricted owing to the rapid electron-hole recombination. In order to improve the photoelectric conversion efficiency of P5FIn, our research group has prepared a series of nanocomposites with excellent photoelectric properties by coupling P5FIn with erGO, NiO and Au, such as P5FIn/erGO [26], erGO/P5FIn/Au [20], P5FIn/NiO heterojunctions [27] and P5FIn/Au [28]. Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) with the characteristics of high conductivity, good stability and optical transparency, easy synthesis and large specific surface is an environmentally friendly organic semiconducting polymers, which can capture more photoexcited electrons and reduce the recombination of photogenerated charge due to its conjugation structure [23,29,30]. Whereas, the large band gap of pure PEDOT is not conducive to the exciting of PEDOT by visible light [23]. Combining PEDOT with P5FIn with narrow band gap is expected to obtain an organic polymer heterojunction with matching energy level structure, thus improving the shortage of single material. Compared with majority of inorganic semiconductor, PEDOT has superior conductivity, wide visible light absorption range and larger specific surface area [31], which is favorable to increase the electron transfer rate and facilitate the absorption and utilization of visible light of P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction, thus enhancing the photoelectric properties of photosensitive materials.

The photoactive materials and the biorecognition elements are usually fixed on the same electrode in some traditional photocathode PEC biosensors, which may cause serious damage to the photoactive materials due to the complex process of biological modification, and thus reducing the sensitivity and stability of the sensor [32,33]. At the same time, the complex material modification process on single electrode surface can also cause the differences between sensors, resulting in poor reproducibility of sensors [34]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a new kind of photocathode PEC biosensor with better stability, sensitivity and reproducibility. Song et al. designed a selectivity-enhanced PEC enzyme sensor by integrating photoanode and biocathode [35]. The composite photoanode photoelectric active materials and enzyme were placed on two different electrodes in their work, which avoided the direct contact between bioactive materials and photoactive materials and effectively improved the selectivity and sensitivity of PEC sensor. In order to further enhance the cathode photocurrent response, a feasible strategy of modifying photoanode materials as counter electrode and photocathode materials as working electrode was developed. For example, Tian et al. fabricated a double photoelectrode system for nifedipine detection, in which the ITO/CuO/CYP3A4 photocathode was used as working electrode and the TiO2/g-C3N4 photoanode was used as the counter electrode. The fabricated double photoelectrode system had a high catalytic activity toward substrate nifedipine [36]. Liu et al. developed a dual electrode-based PEC aptasensor using Fe2O3/AgInS2 as the photoanode and the BiOI nanosphere as the photocathode for 17β-estradiol detection, which showed a wide detection range and a low detection limit [34]. Inspired by the strategy of integrating anode and cathode, it is expected to improve the stability and sensitivity of the photocathode PEC sensor by separating the photocathode photoelectric active materials and the biometric components. However, the cathode photocurrent in the above study is mainly generated by the photoanode material as the counter electrode. Moreover, in order to increase the photocurrent response, the photocathode as the detection electrode also needs to receive light irradiation. This has negative impact on the biological active substances in the sensor. It is likely that the biological active substances will be inactivated by light irradiation, thus affecting the performance of the sensor. Hence, the photocathode material is used as the working electrode to produce the cathode photocurrent directly. At the same time, the counter electrode is used as the probe electrode for object detection. As it has no photoelectric activity, the counter electrode does not need to be exposed to light irradiation during PEC detection, which can effectively avoid the influence of light irradiation on biological active substances. This method is an effective strategy to improve the performance of PEC sensor containing biological active substances.

Based on the above discussion, herein, a separated type cathode PEC (STC-PEC) aptasensor was proposed based on P5FIn/PEDOT organic polymer heterojunction for the TB detection, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction as working electrode was synthesized directly on the surface of ITO electrode by two-step electropolymerization and then the prepared P5FIn/PEDOT/ITO electrode was used as photocathode. The aptamer/electrochemically reduced graphene oxide/ITO (aptamer/erGO/ITO) electrode was used as biodetection electrode which was prepared by fixing TB aptamers on the surface of erGO through π–π stacking interaction [20]. TB aptamers can stay away from the surface of biodetection electrode through the specific recognition and binding between aptamers and TB, which can reduce the electron transmission barrier on the interface of biodetection electrode–electrolyte, and then enhancing the photocurrent signal. Impressively, it is proved that the PEC sensor has great applicability in the detection of TB in human serum.
All of the reagents and apparatus involved in this experiment are described in Supporting Information .
Specific details of the fabrication of P5FIn/PEDOT/ITO photocathode are given in Supporting Information.
Specific details of the fabrication of aptamer/erGO/ITO biodetection electrode are described in the Supporting Information.

Before the PEC measurement, the as-prepared aptamer/erGO/ITO electrode was incubated with different concentrations TB for 90 min at 37 °C, followed by washing with deionized water. Then the PEC measurement was performed in a three-electrode system. The aptamer/erGO/ITO electrode, the as-prepared P5FIn/PEDOT/ITO photocathode and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter electrode, working electrode and reference electrode, respectively, which were all placed in 0.100 M PBS solution (pH 7.4). The dissolved O2 in PBS solution was served as the electron acceptors. The time interval of light on and light off was 20 s. 20 cycles of optical conversion were performed and 0.0 V was set as the bias potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of P5FIn/PEDOT organic polymer heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological structures of erGO, PEDOT, P5FIn and P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction on the ITO electrodes were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) in Fig. 1 . The erGO had obvious fold-like thin layer structure (Fig. 1A), which not only greatly increased specific surface area for more TB aptamers attaching but also improved the conductivity of biometric electrode. Fig. 1B showed that PEDOT film had a spongelike porous network morphology and a large surface area, which was favorable to loading more P5FIn [37]. P5FIn had three-dimensional porous network structure and many independent protrusions (Fig. 1C). P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction exhibited porous and protrusions structures similar to P5FIn. However, compared with P5FIn, P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction had smaller pore size and rougher surface (Fig. 1D). This indicated that P5FIn was successfully decorated on the skeleton of PEDOT. The three-dimensional porous network structure and rough surface state of P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction were helpful to facilitate the absorption and utilization of visible light due to the large specific surface area [23]. The electrochemical properties of P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction were also characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV). As shown in Fig. 2 A and B, the integral area of CV curve of P5FIn gradually increased with the increasing cycle number, which indicated that the amount of P5FIn continuously increased on the bare ITO electrode and PEDOT/ITO electrode. While the increasing oxidation peak potential may be caused by the raised contact resistance during film growth [27]. In addition, the redox peak and peak current of P5FIn were slightly improved in Fig. 2B as compared with Fig. 2A, which showed that the polymerization effect of 5FIn on PEDOT substrate is better than that on bare ITO. Usually, the first circle of CV scan was regarded as characteristic CV in the nucleation process of polymer on electrode surface, which was of great significance to study the properties of polymer. As shown in Fig. 2C, the polymerization potentials of 5FIn on ITO electrode (curve a) and PEDOT/ITO electrode (curve b) were respectively about 1.3 V and 1.05 V, showing that the electrochemical polymerization of 5FIn was easier to proceed on PEDOT/ITO electrode. The lower polymerization potential could also inhibit the occurrence of side reactions during the electrochemical polymerization process. Moreover, the tenth CV scan of P5FIn on ITO electrode (curve a) and PEDOT/ITO electrode (curve b) was used to investigate the polymerization effect of P5FIn on different electrodes. As shown in Fig. S1, the peak current of curve b was larger than that of curve a, showing an enhanced electrochemical activity of P5FIn on PEDOT/ITO electrode. That might be mainly due to two reasons. Firstly, the electrochemical activity of PEDOT/ITO was better than bare ITO, which was conducive to the electrochemical polymerization of 5FIn on PEDOT/ITO. Secondly, the specific surface area of ITO electrode modified by PEDOT was increased, which could load more P5FIn. In addition, Fig. 2D showed that the P5FIn/PEDOT nanocomposite had the redox peaks of PEDOT and P5FIn, suggesting that the P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction had the electrochemical redox characteristics of both PEDOT and P5FIn. Meanwhile, the peak shape of P5FIn/PEDOT was wider than that of pure PEDOT and P5FIn, indicating that the electrochemical activity of P5FIn/PEDOT was enhanced. In short, all of these CV results showed that the electrochemical polymerization of 5FIn was easier to proceed on PEDOT/ITO electrode than bare ITO electrode and the electrochemical performance of P5FIn/PEDOT nanocomposites was more excellent than that of pure P5FIn.

The photoelectric properties of photoactive materials are crucial to the sensitivity of the sensors. Thus, the PEC tests were performed on PEDOT, P5FIn and P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction to further explore their photoelectric performance. As shown in Fig. 3 A, under the illumination of visible light, almost no photocurrent signal was generated by PEDOT (curve a). P5FIn generated a photocurrent signal of 3 µA (curve b) and the P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction produced a strong photocurrent signal of 11 µA (curve c). These results indicated that the photoelectric properties of P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction were better than that of pure P5FIn and PEDOT. The photocurrent response of P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction was about three times stronger than that of pure P5FIn, confirming the synergic effect between P5FIn and PEDOT. In addition, the light absorption characteristics of P5FIn, PEDOT and P5FIn/PEDOT are also studied. As shown in Fig. S4, pure PEDOT has a wide absorption in visible region. And pure P5FIn has no obvious absorption at 550–800 nm. And its absorbance increases obviously when the wavelength is less than 500 nm. For P5FIn/PEDOT, the absorption between 460 and 800 nm is significantly enhanced compared with P5FIn and PEDOT. This result indicates that the combination of P5FIn and PEDOT effectively improves the visible light utilization. Therefore, the excellent photoelectric properties of P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction might be explained from the following three aspects: (i) The stepped energy level structure was formed after the combination of P5FIn and PEDOT, which could promote the rapid transfer of electrons and reduce the electron-hole recombination. The detailed calculation process of the optical band gaps and energy levels of PEDOT and P5FIn can be find in the Supporting Information. (ii) The electrochemical activity of P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction was enhanced due to the synergistic enhancement effect of PEDOT-based materials, which can facilitate electronics transport during the redox reactions [30]. (iii) The large specific surface area of P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction was beneficial to promote the absorption and utilization of visible light and improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thus increasing the photocurrent intensity [23].

According to the calculated optical band gap and energy level of PEDOT and P5FIn, the electron transfer mechanism diagram of STC-PEC aptasensor was depicted (Fig. 3B). Under visible light irradiation, P5FIn/PEDOT heterojunction were excited and a large number of electron-hole pairs were generated. The photogenerated electrons generated by PEDOT were more easily transferred to P5FIn, since the LUMO and HOMO energy level of P5FIn and PEDOT presented a stepped distribution. And then the dissolved oxygen near the interface of the photoelectrode-electrolyte solution was reduced to oxygen negative ions through capturing the photogenerated electrons. These oxygen negative ions can release electrons on the surface of biodetection electrode and reconvert to dissolved oxygen [28,38]. While these released electrons will be reinjected into the photogenerated holes of the P5FIn/PEDOT nanocomposites through an external circuit, thereby completing an electron transfer cycle process. Due to the separation of photocathode and biorecognition elements, the damage to photoactive materials caused by complex biological modification process is avoided. At the same time, the negative influence caused by illumination in the detection process of biological active substances is also avoided. Therefore, the separable design strategy can effectively improve stability, sensitivity and reproducibility of the sensor.

In the PEC process of photocathodic biosensors, dissolved O2 usually acts as an electron acceptor to be reduced [39]. Since dissolved oxygen acts as the electron acceptor during photocurrent generation, the generated oxygen negative ions may affect the modified photoactive material surface in the PEC detection process. In order to explain whether dissolved oxygen can affect the electrode modification material, the long-term photocurrent stability of P5FIn/PEDOT was also studied. Fig. S5 was the long-term photocurrent response of the P5FIn/PEDOT/ITO photocathode. Upon 24 cycles of repeated light illumination, the photocurrent intensity was almost unchanged, indicating high stability of P5FIn/PEDOT/ITO electrode. Furthermore, these photoactive materials did not fall off from the electrode after a long-term cycle of testing. These results showed that the dissolved oxygen had little effect on the photoactive material surface in the PEC detection process. And the good stability of P5FIn/PEDOT was suitable for establishing a photocathode PEC sensor.

In the constructed STC-PEC aptasensor, TB aptamers could be immobilized on the erGO surface through π-π stacking and hinder electron transmission [40]. When the aptamer/erGO/ITO electrode was incubated with TB, aptamers could preferentially bind with TB and detach from erGO, which reduced the barrier of electron transfer on the surface of biodetection electrode and enhanced the photocurrent signal. Thus, the sensitive and selective detection of TB can be realized.

In order to verify the successful construction of the proposed STC-PEC aptasensor, the photocurrent characterization (Fig. 4 A) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 4B) were performed to characterize its construction process. As shown in Fig. 4A, when erGO/ITO electrode was used as counter electrode, the bare ITO electrode had almost no photocurrent signal (curve a). The P5FIn/PEDOT/ITO electrode showed a cathode photocurrent signal of up to 12 µA (curve b). After the aptamers were dropped on erGO/ITO electrode, the photoelectric signal was greatly declined (curve c) because of the poor electron transport capacity of aptamers [27]. However, when the aptamer/erGO/ITO biodetection electrode was incubated with TB, the photocurrent signal was enhanced (curve d). This was because the aptamers were released from the surface of erGO, reducing the hindrance of photogenerated electron transfer. With the increase of TB content, the number of the aptamer released from erGO surface increased due to the specific binding of TB with its aptamer, resulting in the continuous reduction of electron transfer barrier on the surface of detection electrode and the gradual enhancement of photoelectric current [41]. Therefore, the photocurrent signal gradually increased with the increasing concentration of TB, so as to realize the sensitive detection of TB. Since the constructed PEC sensor don’t need bias voltage during PEC detection in this study and the electrode potential also don’t need to know in the detection process, the two-electrode system can also realize the detection of the target. The photocurrent response (Fig. S6) and the feasibility discussion of two-electrode system are illustrated in the support information.

EIS is an effective tool to characterize the interface properties of electrodes. The fitting circuit of the Nyquist plots is showed in the inset of Fig. 4B, which contains the Warburg impedance (Zw), the electron transfer resistance (Ret), the resistance of solution (Rs), and the double layer capacitance (Cdl). The semicircle in impedance spectra represents electron-transfer resistance in equivalent circuit. And the larger the semicircle diameter is, the larger the Ret value is. As shown in Fig. 4B, the impedance of the erGO/ITO was very small (curve a), indicating that erGO had a good electron transfer ability. When the aptamers were fixed on the erGO/ITO electrode, the impedance value of aptamer/erGO/ITO electrode was obviously increased (curve b) because the steric hindrance of aptamers hindered the transfer of electrons and increased the impedance of the electrode surface [42]. After the incubation of TB, the impedance value was decreased markedly (curve c) due to the detaching of aptamers from biodetection electrode by the special binding between aptamers and TB. All of these obtained results were consistent with expectations, indicating the successful construction of the developed STC-PEC aptasensor.

In order to obtain the best detection conditions of the constructed STC-PEC aptasensor, the maximum load of aptamers and the incubation time of TB were optimized. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the photocurrent signal continuously decreased as the concentration of aptamers increasing when the aptamers were modified on the surface of detection electrode. And the photocurrent signal was almost unchanged after the concentration of aptamers was increased to 5.00 µM. Therefore, 5.00 µM was the optimal concentration of aptamers incubated on the biodetection electrode. In addition, the incubation time of TB on the surface of biodetection electrode also played an important role in the detection process of the recommended sensor. As shown in Fig. 5B, the photocurrent signal increased with the increasing of incubation time in the first 90 min but kept no significant increases when further increased the incubation time. Therefore, 90 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time of TB on the detection electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the STC-PEC aptasensor for TB
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the analytical performance of this STC-PEC aptasensor for TB, the biodetection electrode was incubated with various concentrations of TB to perform PEC measurement under the optimal detection conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the photocurrent signal gradually increased with the increasing concentration of TB (curve a → k). Moreover, the excellent linear relationship was demonstrated between the absolute value of photocurrent signal and the logarithm of TB concentration in Fig. 6B. The linear regression equation was |I| = 0.3983 lg(C thrombin /nM) + 9.7486, with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9954. In addition, the detection limit of proposed sensor was calculated to be 0.0410 pM by using the 3σ method. Furthermore, the proposed sensor could be comparable to or even more excellent than some published TB sensors (listed in Table 1 ) due to its wider detection range and lower detection limit. Such good detection performance of our proposed sensor could be attributed to the following aspects: Firstly, the sensitivity of this sensor could be improved by strong photocurrent response caused by P5FIn/PEDOT nanocomposites with an outstanding photoelectric activity. Secondly, the mode of separating the photocathode from the biodetection electrode avoided the adverse interaction between photosensitive materials and bioactive materials, and kept cathode photocurrent signal consistent. This made the sensitivity and stability of this sensor enhanced remarkably. Finally, the detection strategy of “signal on” can reduce false positive signal and further increase the analysis effect of suggested sensor [43]. Thus, the constructed STC-PEC aptasensor exhibited an excellent detection performance for TB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, selectivity and reproducibility of STC-PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of this STC-PEC aptasensor was estimated with 0.0100 nM TB in 400 s (light switching was performed at 20 s intervals). As show in Fig. 7 A, the photocurrent signal was hardly change in the PEC measurement when the lamp was turned on, showing the outstanding stability of this sensor. At the same time, the selectivity of this sensor was explored with CEA, BSA and IgG as interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 7B, the photocurrent signals of 10.0 nM CEA, BSA, IgG and blank samples were no evident difference, which were obviously lower than that of 0.0100 nM TB. While the signal intensity of 0.0100 nM TB and the mixed solution of interfering substances including 0.0100 nM TB was not fluctuated significantly. This result indicated that the STC-PEC aptasensor had an excellent selectivity for TB, which should be attributed to the specific binding between TB and TB aptamers. In addition, the reproducibility of this sensor was also investigated. Two group electrodes (5/group) coming from the same batch were selected to perform PEC tests on TB with concentrations of 0.0100 nM and 0.100 nM. The relative standard deviations (RSD) of the measurement results were 2.4% and 2.8%, respectively (Fig. 7C). Meanwhile, two sets of electrodes (5/group) of different batches were also tested with the same TB concentration and test conditions, and the relative standard deviations of 3.3% and 3.8% were get. These results indicated that the reproducibility of this STC-PEC aptasensor was satisfied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of STC-PEC aptasensor in real human serum samples detection
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of this STC-PEC aptasensor was investigated through a standard addition method. First, the real human serum samples were diluted with PBS solution (pH 7.4). Then the TB with different concentrations were added in five of above-diluted solutions. Finally, the aptamer/erGO/ITO electrodes were incubated with these as-prepared samples, respectively, before photocurrent signal assay. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rate of TB detection by this sensor was 98.1% −104.0%, and RSD was between 1.1% and 4.1%, indicating that the proposed STC-PEC aptasensor had prominent applicability for the detection of TB in actual samples.

A novel STC-PEC aptasensor for TB detection is constructed with P5FIn/PEDOT/ITO as photocathode and aptamer/erGO/ITO as biodetection electrode. P5FIn/PEDOT organic polymer heterojunction has strong photocurrent response under visible light, which makes up for the defect of single material and improves the sensitivity of this sensor. The strategy of separating the biological detection process from the photoexcitation process avoids the adverse effects of bioactive substances in the photocathode interface reaction and improves the sensitivity, stability and selectivity of the proposed sensor. Simultaneously, the photocathode need not to be replaced in the PEC measurement due to the independent photocathode and biodetection electrode, which improves the reproducibility of this sensor by keeping the photocurrent background signal consistent. The STC-PEC aptasensor has a well linear detection range of 0.000100 nM–10.0 nM, a low detection limit of 0.0410 pM and an excellent applicability in the detection of TB in real samples. This facile strategy of separating photocathode from biological detection electrode provides a new way for the construction of PEC biosensor, which is expected to promote the further development of PEC detection technology.

Ya Tian: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Baoying Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jingjing Wang: Investigation, Validation. Qingfu Guo: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoyan Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Guangming Nie: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51973102); Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2019MB067); Qinghai Provincial Basic Research Program (2021-ZJ-710); Open Foundation of State Key Laboratory Base for Eco-chemical Engineering (STHG2003); and Talent Fund of QUST (2020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.107140.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
